title: Caller passes a named parameter that target function does not declare
description: A function is invoked with a -Param value that the target function's param() block does not declare, raising the runtime error 'A parameter cannot be found that matches parameter name <Name>'. Common cause: the caller assumes a public/conventional knob (e.g. -Prefix, -Title, -Color) that was never wired into the producer, or the producer accepts the value only via an internal scriptblock setter (e.g. SetText) but not as a constructor parameter. This often surfaces only at GUI-construction time inside catch-handled try blocks, so it is invisible until the surrounding feature path runs.
detection_method: Static cross-reference of internal function call sites against their param() blocks. For each `Verb-Noun -ParamName` invocation of a function defined in this workspace, verify ParamName exists in the function's param() declaration (case-insensitive, allowing partial-prefix match per PowerShell binder rules).
preventionRule: When extending a caller to pass a new named parameter, first confirm the target function declares that parameter in its param() block. If the value is only settable via a returned scriptblock (e.g. SetText), either invoke that scriptblock after construction or add the parameter to the constructor with a default that preserves prior behavior. Never rely on the parameter binder to silently swallow unknown named arguments — it does not.
remedy: Two equivalent fixes: (A) Add the missing parameter to the target function's param() block with a sensible default — preferred when the value is naturally a constructor knob. (B) Remove the unsupported argument from the call site and instead invoke the post-construction setter (e.g. & $obj.SetText 'Processing'). Bump the module VersionTag after either fix.

first_instance.fix_summary: Added [string]$Prefix='Processing' parameter to New-SpinnerLabel; threaded into $spinState.Prefix initialiser.
